name: Knowledge2 ICP
bottom_line: Pre-2025 incumbent software companies with proprietary workflow/data assets, enough customers to feel competitive pressure, and either no public AI narrative or a shallow AI feature that does not yet change the customer's core workflow.
description: This is the Knowledge2 ICP, shipped as the default — edit it to match your own ICP. The model has four parts: (1) hard gates, each a boolean the account MUST pass — one failure rejects it regardless of score; (2) graded scoring dimensions, each worth up to max_points — the classify skill awards partial points with evidence; (3) tier thresholds on the normalized 0-100 score; (4) signals — keyword groups the enrich stage scans for on the website/careers/GitHub, each mapped via `informs` to the dimension it scores (e.g. hiring LangChain engineers informs commercial_urgency). priority_verticals are a hint for discover/classify, not a gate.

gates.description: Founded before the current AI-native wave — an incumbent with customers, not a brand-new startup.
Sells a product, not primarily services or consulting.
B2B or B2B2C — business customers, enterprise accounts, or partner channels.
Owns proprietary workflow/data (transactions, tickets, inventory, claims, schedules, documents, etc.).
Roughly 25-2000 employees, or smaller if clearly funded — enough budget to buy.
AI is not the founding premise or core category — leaves room for an AI gap to close.

dimensions.description: Distance between today's product and what AI could do for it — bigger gap, bigger opportunity.
Depth and defensibility of the proprietary data/workflow the product sits on.
Competitive or market pressure pushing them to act now (funding, churn, new entrants).
Ability to fund a deal and reachability of an economic buyer.
How tractable a first engagement looks — data accessible, scope bounded.

anti_icp.description: Red flags are soft disqualifiers — unlike a hard gate (one failure rejects), a single red flag is tolerated, but `deprioritize_at` or more co-occurring red flags demote the account to Nurture regardless of score. classify judges each `present: true|false` with evidence, mirroring gates.

red_flags.description: Has publicly rebuilt the core product around AI / repositioned as AI-native — the gap is already closing without us.
Recently PE-acquired into a cost-cutting/rollup posture — opex frozen, no appetite for new platform spend.
No real engineering team (outsourced dev shop, or pure reseller) — can't integrate or sustain an AI build.
Already committed to a competing AI platform or a recent in-house build — no room to land.
Layoffs, sunset announcements, or acquisition-in-progress — buying motion frozen.

signals.interpretation: Open roles for AI/LLM skills (e.g. hiring LangChain engineers) = the company feels AI pressure and is building in-house. Raises commercial urgency and confirms the AI gap is real but not yet productized.
Public AI product language. Thin or marketing-only mentions (no paid SKU, no case study) widen the ai_gap; deep, productized AI narrows it.
Operational-workflow and data-integration language signals a proprietary data/workflow moat to ground AI on.

positioning.description: Seller context for the personalize stage. value_pillars are what we actually win on (lead the rewrite on one, backed by proof); do_not_say are forbidden claims — overpromises, competitor bashing, unverifiable numbers — every draft is checked against them before sending.

value_pillars.name: You already own the data
value_pillars.proof: The workflow/transaction data the AI needs is yours — no buying, no cold start.

value_pillars.name: Grounded, not generative guesswork
value_pillars.proof: Answers cite your records; we right-size retrieval to your corpus instead of hand-waving an LLM.

value_pillars.name: First AI feature in weeks, not a platform rebuild
value_pillars.proof: We map one high-value surface and ship against your real product, not a slideware roadmap.

ai_traction_checks: no paid AI SKU or add-on
no AI case studies or named-customer adoption proof
few/no AI mentions in third-party reviews
AI features lack action-capable workflow depth
weak data grounding behind any AI feature
